limitations: The verb lexicon is English-centric. A tool named with a non-English verb (e.g. `supprimer`, `borrar`) will not match the name-verb signals and may derive as `uncertain`.
The verb lexicon is alias-incomplete. Uncommon spellings and compounded names (e.g. `rm_node`, `nuke`, `yeet`) are not enumerated and may be missed.
Name and description signals are natural language and therefore inherently ambiguous; schema-shape signals are language-independent and are weighted more heavily to compensate.
This is a v0 seed. Coverage of read/create/mutate/delete/send/transfer families and the two highest-signal schema shapes is deliberate; breadth grows by evidence-backed contribution, not by guessing.

Name-verb and parameter-name signals match exact tokens after case and separator normalization; the data cannot see negation or prefixing. A negated description ("does not permanently delete") still matches a deletion phrase — one reason description-phrase weights are low — and a prefixed name (`undelete`) simply fails to match, resolving conservatively to uncertain.

`query` is deliberately excluded from the free-form-code-input parameter names: it names benign natural-language search input far more often than executable code, and search tools are the most common benign tool class. `sql`, `statement`, `command`, and `script` carry the code-input claim instead.

entries.evidence: Retrieval verbs denote reading state without changing it; a tool named this way is a strong read-only signal.
entries.citation: MCP tool annotations, readOnlyHint (Model Context Protocol specification, 2025-03-26).

entries.evidence: Creation verbs add new state; repeating the call typically creates another instance, so the action is not naturally idempotent.
Mutation verbs change existing state in place; a tool named this way overwrites a value that was there before.
entries.citation: MCP tool annotations, destructiveHint (Model Context Protocol specification, 2025-03-26).

entries.evidence: Deletion and revocation verbs remove state or access; recovery is not guaranteed by the action itself.
entries.citation: MCP tool annotations, destructiveHint (Model Context Protocol specification, 2025-03-26).

entries.evidence: Communication verbs emit information beyond the local boundary; a sent message is externally visible and cannot be unsent, and repeating the call sends again.
entries.citation: MCP tool annotations, openWorldHint (Model Context Protocol specification, 2025-03-26).

entries.evidence: Payment verbs move value. Once settled, the movement cannot be undone by the tool itself, repeating the call moves value again, and settlement typically involves an external system.
entries.citation: Formael Capability Risk Profile — monetary sensitivity mapping.

entries.evidence: Transfer verbs move an asset between parties, and when the asset is money, ownership, or access the movement is hard to reverse. The verb also names benign relocations — files, calls — so it is a supporting signal rather than a decisive one.
Execution verbs let a caller drive behavior the annotations rarely capture; conservatively treated as at least mutating, since the specific effect is not knowable from the name.
Prose that mentions permanent or irreversible removal is a weak supporting signal — it corroborates a name-verb reading but is not decisive on its own.
Prose describing outbound communication weakly supports open-world reach; the schema-shape destination signal is the stronger, language-independent evidence.

An unconstrained free-form string parameter interpreted as code (a command, script, or SQL statement with no enum, format, or tight pattern) lets a caller drive arbitrary behavior; conservatively treated as at least mutating, and its reach cannot be determined from the shape alone.
entries.citation: Practical-DevSecOps MCP server security misconfigurations audit guide — free-form input interpreted as code is among the two most common critical findings in a 100+ production-server survey.

entries.evidence: A string parameter whose declared format is a URI, an email address, or a hostname is structured, self-describing evidence that the tool addresses something beyond the local boundary.
entries.citation: MCP tool annotations, openWorldHint (Model Context Protocol specification, 2025-03-26).

entries.evidence: A parameter named like a communication destination — a URL, webhook, recipient, or channel — suggests the tool can reach outside the local boundary; a bare name is weaker evidence than a declared format.
